data.title: goeasy's Investment Thesis Got Crushed Overnight, Don't Buy The Dip
data.description:
goeasy (GSY:CA) (EHMEF), a non-prime lender based in Canada, shocked many investors on March 10 when it released a pre-earnings financial update that was much worse than people expected. The stock crashed 57% on the day of the announcement and has fallen 69% in total since March 9, as the investment thesis got crushed overnight.

I'll go over the update in more detail later on, but in short:

  • Charge-offs (loans that are written off) and net increases in allowance for credit losses are going to be higher than expected in Q4.
  • The company's accounting practices proved to be too aggressive.
  • goeasy withdrew its three-year outlook while suspending dividends and buybacks.

As someone who first bought goeasy six years ago during the pandemic crash and first covered goeasy in 2020 and last covered it in 2024, this is extremely disappointing. My last article from November 2024 was titled "goeasy: Why Double-Digit Annual Returns Are Likely," but it's clear that the title should have stated "negative double-digit annual returns" instead.

Now, many of you are probably wondering whether you should buy the dip or not. My answer, personally, is a big fat "no." In fact, I think it's a sell.

Dips can make sense to buy when the price drops while the thesis is still intact, but the thesis changed. This used to be a dividend stock with a relatively high yield and double-digit dividend and EPS growth as well. Now, it's more of a turnaround play, with a potential turnaround being too far out and highly uncertain.

While the company mentioned a 6-point action plan to strengthen the business, management's credibility has taken a hit, and it's hard to reliably estimate how that action plan will turn out.

In the meantime, there aren't any real catalysts to push the stock higher, and we still have to hear what management has to say during the Q4 earnings call after it reports earnings on March 25.

Unless your investing/trading style is buying into turnaround plays very early or hoping for short-term oversold bounces, holding onto GSY:CA stock doesn't make sense to me. I give it a sell rating. Save your dip-buying money for easier opportunities.

The News That Caused goeasy To Go South

The company started its press release by saying that it "expects to incur an incremental charge-off in Q4 2025 of approximately $178M against gross consumer loans receivable of $5.5B as of December 31, 2025, and a related write-down of approximately $55M for loan interest and fees. Total Company net charge-offs (including the incremental charge-off above) in the quarter are expected to be approximately $331M."

Following that statement, goeasy mentioned that its allowance for credit losses on gross consumer loans receivable will be $86 million higher in Q4 than they were in Q3 2025.

For the full year, its net charge-off rate is expected to be ~12.9%. For context, its annualized net charge-off rate was 9.1% in Q4 2025 and 8.8% in Q4 2024.

The company then withdrew its Q4 outlook and its three-year forecast. The three-year forecast was quite optimistic, since it forecasted growth, 23%+ returns on equity from 2025-2027, increasing operating margins, and net charge-offs in the high-single digits. It makes complete sense why the stock fell so much after it withdrew this optimistic guidance without any prior warning.

Another killer was the suspension of the buyback plan and the dividend. The annualized dividend used to be $5.84/share. Before the crash, that meant the yield was over 5%. And since the company generally announced dividend increases along with its Q4 results, I'm sure many people were expecting a dividend increase, not a complete suspension.

But What Actually Caused The Financials To Crumble?

goeasy's LendCare business, which it acquired in 2021, was the main culprit. LendCare provides financing solutions mostly in the auto and powersports categories, but it's not going so well right now. The $178 million in expected incremental charge-off is due to loans from this company. Management expects LendCare's poor results to also lead to an annual net charge-off rate in the mid-teens this year before improving in 2027 and beyond.

On top of that, management is going to revise LendCare's reporting practices.

Specifically, "The historical reporting practice resulted in certain customer payments being recorded as received while they were in fact in the process of being settled at month end, some of which were ultimately not collected, and also impacted the company's reported delinquencies."

Apparently, the impact of this correction won't be material to goeasy's results, but this doesn't exactly make me trust the company.

6-Point Action Plan Isn't Enough To Make Me Buy

Here's management's 6-point plan, directly from its press release, that it believes will strengthen the business:

  • Focusing growth on easyfinancial's unsecured and home equity lending direct-to-consumer business
  • Reducing auto and powersports originations from LendCare's merchant channels
  • Integrating easyfinancial and LendCare into one unified operating model
  • Implementing identified operational efficiencies expected to deliver approximately $30M in annualized cost savings
  • Strengthening leadership at LendCare, including appointing Farhan Ali Khan as the new Head of LendCare
  • Not repurchasing shares under its Normal Course Issuer Bid and suspending its quarterly dividend, effective immediately, until further notice

Everything in the plan makes sense. goeasy wants to focus more on its core business while reducing certain LendCare originations. It also wants to cut costs, strengthen LendCare leadership, and preserve capital by suspending the dividend and buybacks.

I'm not hating on the plan, but there are a few questions in my head: how well can goeasy execute this plan? How long will it take to play out? And why should shareholders trust the company after what just happened?

I Sold As Soon As I Read The News

As soon as I saw the goeasy news, I sold the stock. I didn't hesitate. I'm thankful I only had a small position of 9 shares. I knew to keep it small because I'm generally risk-averse with most financial stocks, as things like this can happen quickly, so I always take that into consideration.

What's funny is that I had been patiently watching GSY:CA stock fall for a while (I had sold it near its highs in September 2025), and I finally re-entered with a small position on March 4 at a price of $106.71. Initially, that seemed to be a good price because it was a swing low on the chart, and the stock had a 5%+ dividend yield with a positive three-year outlook.

But when the company's credibility, future outlook, and dividend were thrown out the window, I said to myself, "What reason do I have to hold onto this stock?" I found no reason, and essentially sold right away at $62.57. With the stock at $35.72 now, at least I saved myself from further losses.

Earnings Estimates Got Hammered

Right now, you may look at goeasy's EPS estimates and forward valuation and conclude that this is a great value play. If analysts' estimates are correct, then the stock trades at just 3.4x its expected 2027 earnings, and when (if) the company reinstates its dividend, you'd likely be sitting on a high yield. That can happen, and that's essentially the bull case from here.

However, the main issue is that we don't know how reliable these estimates really are, especially this early on in the turnaround process.

If you look at the EPS revision trend, you'll see just how much the estimates have been reset. Just one month ago, the 2025, 2026, and 2027 EPS estimates were $16.33, $20.09, and $23.59, respectively.

Now, they're $3.46, $5.07, and $10.31, respectively.

Analysts were way off the first time, and they can be way off again.

My Final Thoughts On GSY:CA Stock

We all make mistakes in the stock market. The key point is to not let a mistake destroy your account. Keep risky positions at a manageable size, and get out when the thesis changes. Don't keep holding in hopes of a turnaround that you can't predict when there are thousands of other stocks out there to choose from. Of course, this is just my opinion, not professional advice.

I'm going to pay attention to what management says in its upcoming earnings report, and I'll look for improvements in the coming quarters, especially regarding EPS revisions. I want to see there be a "floor" for EPS expectations because we have no idea what that floor is right now.

Either way, with the dividend gone and my trust in the company gone as well, I say farewell, goeasy. It's been a nice run for the past 6 years being in and out of the stock, but it's time to move on to better companies.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: CEF Market Weekly Review: Daily-NAV CEFs Point To More Pain For CLO Equity
data.description:
Welcome to another installment of our CEF Market Weekly Review, where we discuss closed-end fund [CEF] market activity from both the bottom-up - highlighting individual fund news and events - as well as the top-down - providing an overview of the broader market. We also try to provide some historical context as well as the relevant themes that look to be driving markets or that investors ought to be mindful of.

This update covers the period through the first week of March. Be sure to check out our other weekly updates covering the business development company [BDC] as well as the preferreds/baby bond markets for perspectives across the broader income space.

Market Action

It was a difficult week for CEFs, with all sectors finishing in the red. EM funds led to the downside.

Fixed-income sector discounts weakened more than equity sector CEFs over the last week.

The last bout of marked anxiety pushed the median CEF sector discount past its longer-term average.

Market Themes

CLO Equity has been under pressure over the past year. This was despite relatively well-behaved credit markets, even if there was an odd cockroach or two now and again.

Our estimates suggest that February may be a particularly difficult month for the sector (nearly all CEFs in the sector report NAVs on a monthly basis and are yet to report the February numbers). Current estimates of February NAVs in our CEF Tool are a double-digit drop. These estimates are derived from a regression relationship with XFLT which does report on a daily basis and holds about a third of the portfolio in CLO Equity. For example, this is what the monthly NAV changes look like for XFLT and OCCI.

With February being the worst month for XFLT for a long time, we are worried about the fallout across the broader CLO Equity CEF space. Of course there could be an issue idiosyncratic to XFLT such as specific portfolio positioning; however, ARDC, which has a 10% CLO Equity allocation, also had one of its worst NAV months as well in February. The common theme appears to be weakness in the broader syndicated loan market, precipitated by the AI disruption theme of the software sector.

Ultimately, both the tightness in loan spreads (i.e., high loan prices) and volatility in loan prices that we have seen over the last few weeks, particularly in the software sector, are headwinds for the sector. An environment of wide loan spreads amid modest volatility / low defaults where CLO Equity could shine may not be on the cards for some time, though it’s impossible to tell, and we could see a bounceback next month.

Until the recent decline in loan prices, the primary catalyst for CLO Equity underperformance has been what are called captive CLO funds. Because these funds internalize fees, they can print CLOs at a less favorable arbitrage level than would be accepted by third-party CLO Equity managers. This means that captive fund activity puts significant downward pressure on loan spreads and reduces the income of CLO Equity securities which are highly sensitive to this due to their annuity-like profile.

If CLO Equity funds do post bad February NAVs, price behavior will likely be very messy, likely driving fund prices to new lows. The extent and relentlessness of the CLO Equity sell-off mean that even funds like XFLT and EIC which have minority allocations to the sector, will not remain immune. It also means that it’s hard to maintain conviction in the sector, as there are no obvious limits to how long the sell-off can carry on. This is different from traditional credit assets, which have well-tested historical ranges of credit spreads. It’s also different from a sector like BDCs, where performance has remained strong despite poor news flow.

Market Commentary

It was another quiet month for CEF distributions. There were the usual small adjustments for BlackRock ECAT and BCAT. Invesco bond fund VLT slightly cut the distribution, which it also did around 6 months ago. The distribution of $0.0915 remains well above NII of around $0.062 as of the last semi-annual report. NII will rise over the next few months to reflect the lower cost of leverage via lower short-term rates (offset partly by the decline in income on its loan assets).

A couple of John Hancock funds, JHS and JHI cut the distribution by double-digit levels. The distributions of these funds have been all over the place, and the cuts are a reversal of the jump in the previous quarter.

Stance and Takeaways

On the back of XFLT's February struggles, we exited our allocation to both it as well as EIC. We have long steered clear of full CLO Equity CEFs for a number of factors, including their historically rich valuations, likely distribution cuts, and a tendency to deleverage. However, the sheer extent of the sell-off in CLO Equity has left a mark even on partial CLO Equity funds as well, such as XFLT and EIC. For now, the structural shifts in the CLO industry as well as weakness in the syndicated loan market remain key headwinds for the space, with little if any light at the end of the tunnel.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Principal Well-Being Index: Firms Looking Through The Sticker Shock
data.description:
By Seema Shah, Chief Global Strategist | Christian Floro, CFA, CMT, Market Strategist

Price pressures remain top of mind for many firms, fueling the desire to improve profitability through increased business efficiency. As a result, firms have signaled borrowing activity this year will skew towards funding technology upgrades, productivity enhancements, and expansion plans, despite elevated financing costs. This willingness to invest in strategically critical imperatives is reshaping capital allocation decisions.

What initiatives firms will use financing to support this year

Principal Well-Being Index

The latest Principal Well-Being Index indicates that price pressures remain a central concern for businesses. This is prompting firms to focus on extracting efficiency gains, particularly through technological innovation.

The Principal Well-Being Index measures sentiment among small- and medium-sized businesses by asking business decision-makers about current conditions and the economic outlook. The most recent survey, conducted between January 15 and February 1, collected responses from 1,000 firms—on par with the NFIB Small Business Optimism Index in scale.

Results show that the cost of healthcare, employee benefits, and tariffs on foreign goods and materials remain significant concerns across firms of all sizes. In response, businesses are restraining job openings and keeping headcount broadly stable. Simultaneously, many are actively pursuing efficiency improvements, including borrowing to fund technology upgrades, productivity enhancements, and expansion plans, even as financing costs remain elevated. Notably, there is a growing sense of urgency: firms across sectors and size categories report a willingness to borrow at interest rates approaching 12% when investments are viewed as strategically critical.

That willingness points to a structural shift. The imperative to invest in growth while extracting efficiency gains through technology is reshaping capital allocation decisions. As a result, both small and large businesses are increasingly prepared to take on debt to finance investments in software, automation, and digital infrastructure, despite borrowing costs that remain well above pre-pandemic levels.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Columbia Threadneedle Fixed-Income Monitor: March 2026
data.description:
By Samantha Parsons

Determining how to allocate to fixed income requires careful consideration. Our monthly Fixed-Income Monitor consolidates the views of our investment team to help identify opportunities and risks across the asset class.

Key takeaways for March 2026

  • The Federal Reserve kept rates on hold, reinforcing a patient stance as it assesses the balance between moderating growth and still-elevated inflation. While policy projections continue to point to potential easing later in the year, officials emphasized data dependence amid mixed signals on inflation progress and the growth outlook.
  • The Supreme Court's ruling striking down tariffs imposed under emergency powers introduced a new layer of policy uncertainty. Bond markets showed a muted initial reaction, with Treasury yields rising modestly on expectations that the administration would implement tariffs using other legal backing, but ended the month flat. The episode underscored how tariff policy remains a key driver of term premium volatility.
  • Bond yields declined and credit spreads widened over the month, with modest volatility reflecting shifting expectations around the timing of future rate cuts. Softer economic data late in the period supported a retracement in bond yields. Credit spreads widened primarily due to growing uncertainty over the impact of AI technological disruption, with companies deemed most vulnerable seeing the largest impact to credit spreads.
  • Conflict erupted between the U.S./Israel and Iran on February 28. The U.S. dollar rallied as the preferred safe haven. Interest rates rose, mainly due to higher real rates, while inflation expectations lagged despite crude prices increasing.

Yields and credit spread tracker

The proprietary fixed-income tracker compares yields and credit spreads over 20 years of history and across fixed income, which can help investors evaluate how they are being compensated for taking on risk.1

Fixed-income sector positioning

Our fixed-income team analyzes fundamentals, valuations and technicals to assess relative value in bond markets. This approach can help investors position for risks and opportunities across sectors within the asset class.

The bottom line

Fixed income plays a crucial role in a well-diversified portfolio. Our investment team uses bottom-up company and sector analysis with top-down macroeconomic analysis to build fixed-income portfolios. Fixed-income portfolio allocations should reflect personal financial goals and risk tolerance; a financial advisor can help tailor a strategy to fit an individual investor’s needs.

© 2016-2026 Columbia Threadneedle. All rights reserved.

Use of products, materials and services available through Columbia Threadneedle Investments may be subject to approval by your home office.

Investors should consider the investment objectives, risks, charges and expenses of a fund carefully before investing. To learn more about this and other important information about each fund, download a free prospectus. The prospectus should be read carefully before investing.

The views expressed are as of the date given, may change as market or other conditions change and may differ from views expressed by other Columbia Management Investment Advisers, LLC (CMIA) associates or affiliates. Actual investments or investment decisions made by CMIA and its affiliates, whether for its own account or on behalf of clients, may not necessarily reflect the views expressed. This information is not intended to provide investment advice and does not take into consideration individual investor circumstances. Investment decisions should always be made based on an investor's specific financial needs, objectives, goals, time horizon and risk tolerance. Asset classes described may not be appropriate for all investors. Past performance does not guarantee future results, and no forecast should be considered a guarantee either. Since economic and market conditions change frequently, there can be no assurance that the trends described here will continue or that any forecasts are accurate.

Columbia Funds are distributed by Columbia Management Investment Distributors, Inc., member FINRA and are managed by Columbia Management Investment Advisers, LLC. Columbia Management Investment Advisers, LLC serves as the investment manager to the ETFs. ALPS Distributors, Inc. (Member FINRA) is the distributor for Columbia Threadneedle Investments ETFs. Columbia Management Investment Distributors, Inc., (Member FINRA) is a marketing agent for the ETFs. ALPS Distributors, Inc. is not affiliated with Columbia Threadneedle Investments.

Columbia Threadneedle Investments (Columbia Threadneedle) is the global brand name of the Columbia and Threadneedle group of companies.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Market Brief: The AI Agent Wars - What Investors Need To Know
data.description:
Jensen Huang called OpenClaw "probably the most important software release ever." Eight weeks later, the open-source lobster has 163K GitHub stars, its creator has been hired by OpenAI, and every major AI lab is scrambling to ship its own agent platform. This week, that race intensified on multiple fronts. Here's what happened and why it matters for markets.

The Battlefield

Four distinct strategies are now visible.

OpenAI absorbed OpenClaw's creator Peter Steinberger in February and this week acquired Promptfoo, an AI security startup used by over 25% of Fortune 500 companies. The move signals OpenAI's priority: enterprise trust. Promptfoo's red-teaming tools will be integrated into OpenAI Frontier, the company's enterprise agent platform.

Perplexity held its inaugural Ask 2026 developer conference on March 11 and unveiled two agent products. "Perplexity Computer" runs in the cloud; "Personal Computer" runs locally on a dedicated Mac Mini around the clock. Over 100 enterprise customers reportedly reached out over a single weekend after the launch.

NVIDIA (NVDA) is preparing to launch NemoClaw, an open-source enterprise agent framework, at GTC next week (March 17). Unlike OpenClaw's developer-first ethos, NemoClaw targets production environments with built-in audit logs, permission controls, and multi-agent collaboration. NVIDIA has been pitching partnerships to Salesforce (CRM), Google (GOOGL), Adobe (ADBE), and CrowdStrike (CRWD).

Meanwhile, Chinese tech firms have moved fast: Moonshot launched Kimi Claw, Tencent shipped WorkBuddy, and Zhipu AI deployed AutoGLM-OpenClaw on Alibaba Cloud.

The pattern is clear. Value is migrating from the model layer to the orchestration and security layers. Models are becoming interchangeable components; the premium sits in who can coordinate them reliably and safely at enterprise scale.

What It Means for Investors

1 - AI tokens are already trading on the agent narrative

When news of NVIDIA's NemoClaw leaked on March 10, the AI token sector's market cap climbed 6.88% in 24 hours. TAO, NEAR, and ICP all outperformed the CoinDesk 20 index.

The leading movers were TAO, NEAR, and ICP. All three function as high-beta plays on the AI narrative:

  • TAO powers a decentralized machine learning network

  • NEAR is positioning itself as an infrastructure layer for autonomous AI agent transactions

  • ICP enables fully on-chain application deployment.

None of the rallies were driven by project-specific catalysts. Instead, these tokens are behaving as leveraged proxies for AI sector momentum, amplifying moves triggered by events like NVIDIA earnings and agent framework announcements.

These tokens are effectively high-beta proxies for AI sector momentum, amplifying moves triggered by events like NVIDIA earnings and agent framework announcements.

The AI token sector now sits around $16 billion in market cap, and agent-related news is emerging as a distinct tradeable catalyst class separate from broader crypto beta.

2 - On the equities side, the agent wars are reshaping how investors think about AI stocks

NVIDIA remains the infrastructure backbone, with GTC next week as a near-term catalyst. But the more interesting development is Palantir's partnership with NVIDIA announced this week.

The deal bundles NVIDIA's Blackwell Ultra hardware with Palantir's AIP agent platform into a production-ready package, signaling that AI agents are moving beyond the application layer into national-scale infrastructure procurement.

For investors, it reframes the agent narrative: this is no longer just about which chatbot is best, but about who captures the next wave of government and enterprise capex.

3 - Private credit stress adds a macro wrinkle

BlackRock capped withdrawals from its $26B HPS Corporate Lending Fund this week after redemption requests hit 9.3%.

Part of the investor anxiety stems from heavy exposure to software companies that could be disrupted by AI agents. The thesis that agents can replace SaaS workflows is no longer theoretical; it is now a credit risk factor being priced into fund redemptions.

If private credit deleveraging accelerates, it tightens the broader liquidity environment that both crypto and equities trade within.

What to Watch

  • March 17: NVIDIA GTC. Focus on NemoClaw's official launch, enterprise partnership announcements, and any hardware roadmap updates tied to agent workloads.

  • March 17-18: FOMC meeting. A hold is fully priced in. The real question is whether Powell's tone on inflation expectations, now complicated by the oil shock, pushes rate cut timelines further out and dampens risk appetite heading into Q2.

  • Perplexity vs. OpenClaw adoption. Early enterprise feedback on Perplexity Computer will be critical. Can a managed, multi-model agent genuinely match OpenClaw's flexibility in real-world workflows? How users split between the two will signal whether the market favors open-source control or managed convenience.

  • AI token sector correlation. Whether AI tokens continue to decouple from BTC beta on agent-specific catalysts or revert to trading as a high-beta proxy.

The AI wave is moving fast. Whether you're an individual choosing your first personal AI agent or an enterprise evaluating security and compliance, expect a growing roster of agent products tailored to every use case.

But AI's market impact extends far beyond product adoption. Whether you're tracking TAO's price action or BlackRock's next fund filing, the Agent Wars are no longer optional viewing.

Disclaimer: The information provided herein does not constitute investment advice, financial advice, trading advice, or any other sort of advice, and should not be treated as such. All content set out below is for informational purposes only.

Original Post

data.title: Innospec: Oilfield Services Bound For A Recovery
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Given the ongoing war in the Middle East, there has been a worry amongst investors about the oil shortage that could occur in response to the closure of the Strait of Hormuz. Most investors started hitting the bid oil production and refining companies as they'll benefit from the oil price rallying to triple digits in dollar terms, potentially. I see specialty chemical companies like Innospec (IOSP) at an interesting setup to likely benefit from this shortage too. My investment thesis here is that oil-producing and refining companies need fuel additives to increase the quality and the performance of their end product. This includes DRA products that reduce drag as oil passes through pipelines and other additives that reduce corrosion and increase efficiency during the fracking process. Innospec is one of the largest suppliers of such chemicals, and since 2023 top-line growth has disappointed due to softened demand. Just like in 2022 and 2023, when the topline grew due to the energy shortage caused by the Ukraine war, the same is about to happen if this war prolongs.

Between 2022 and 2023, IOSP appreciated by 10% in response to the revenue growth. Given the discounted valuation assigned to Innospec relative to its sector, while being debt-free and having less operational overhead, the stock could rally by 50% if revenue growth recovers.

Since 2023, Innospec has lost about 27.79% of its value, while its closest peer, NewMarket Corporation (NEU), and the S&P 500 returned more than 80%. Despite the firm returning underwhelming top-line growth, I think the stock sell-off was an overreaction given that the fundamentals remain intact. For IOSP to rebound, we need a growth catalyst. That catalyst is the Iranian war that is going to bring back more oil production online to serve the surging demand as the Strait remains in closure, as this will recover the oilfield services, the segment that has been driving revenue contraction in the previous quarters after the loss of a major customer in Mexico.

When we break down the revenue growth by segments, we can see that the growth has mostly been anchored by the performance chemicals segment. Since June 2023, home care sales increased 24% to $109 million, while personal care sales increased 16.45% to $409.2 million. The fuel specialty segment has been flat (-0.71% contraction to $701.5 million) in the same period, while oilfield services declined by 45.46% from the peak at $724.4 million to $395.1 million.

Financial Analysis

Q4 2025 Income Statement

Evaluating the last quarter's results, revenue decreased 2% YoY to $455.6 million, missing estimates by $5.27 million. This revenue decline was mainly caused by oilfield services, home care sales, and other revenue declining by 12%, 2%, and 3%, respectively. Offsetting the decline was fuel specialty growing 1% to $194.1 million and personal care sales also growing 1% to $99.8 million. Cost of revenue decreased 1% to $328.2 million, resulting in gross profits declining 6% to $127.4 million and landing gross margins at 27.96%, slightly underperforming the median sector, which is at 29.82%. Operating expenses decreased 14% to $80.5 million, driven by R&D spending declining by 26% and SG&A spending declining by 12%. As a result, operating income increased 14% to $46.8 million, with EBIT margins at 10.27%, in line with the sector margins of 10.82%. The firm recorded an income tax of $2.9 million, leading to a net income of $47.4 million and an EPS of $1.5, beating estimates by $0.26 on a normalized basis.

FY 2025 Income Statement

Looking at the full-year results, revenue declined 4% to $1.78 billion, with a 19% decline coming from oilfield services, fuel specialty stagnant at $701.5 million, and personal and home care sales increasing 5% and 2%, respectively. Cost of revenue and operating expenses decreased by 1%, leading to gross and operating profit decreasing by 9% and 27%, landing margins at 27.69% and 7.28%, respectively. Income tax increased to $21.5 million, resulting in net income of $116.5 million and net margins of 6.55%, outperforming the sector margins of 4.93%.

Even though performance chemicals have been holding up topline growth, EBIT margins have been volatile and, in 2025, near a record low at 8.95%. Management stated this had to do with continued soft demand from North America and more volume leaning to largely commoditized and low-priced products. In Q1, management guided for EBIT ranging from $10 to $11 million, a 37% QoQ contraction.

Despite the fuel specialties segment consistently returning flat growth quarter after quarter, EBIT margins have expanded significantly since 2023, from 15.76% to 20.64%. In fact, the firm booked a record high EBIT of $37.2 million since 2020. Management attributed the margin growth to low personnel overheads and diversifying its product offering in new markets.

The oilfield segment is the most volatile, as it is the most exposed to the fluctuations of oil & gas prices. And playing into my thesis, we can see that in 2023 the firm recorded the highest EBIT margins since 2014. Given the already surging prices of oil and the commercialization of the firm DRA products, which will boost demand for the oilfield services, I expect margins in 2026 to recover to double digits.

FY2025 Balance Sheet

Innospec has one of the cleanest balance sheets you could find in the specialty chemicals industry. Amongst its peers, it has the lowest debt-to-equity ratio and one of the most liquid books, with a cash balance of $292.5 million. This allows the firm to continue to do dividend payouts and occasionally buy back shares. Even though the firm hasn't bought any shares in the recent quarter, it purchased roughly $22.2 million worth of shares in the full year of 2025, leaving $27.8 million worth of shares to be bought in their $50 million share repurchase program. In 2025, Innospec generated roughly $79 million in free cash flow. This tells you that this debt-free company has enough capabilities to weather the storms of the geopolitical risks and even make strategic acquisitions without having to leverage its balance sheet.

Valuation

Innospec is massively undervalued relative to its sector; on a PE multiple, it trades at a 50% discount (15.12 vs. 22.81), and on the EBITDA multiple, it trades at a 49% discount (7.27 vs. 10.89), even when you account for the 3-year rolling median. I don't see any staying power at these levels given the catalyst events that will likely recover in oilfield services. And even if the stock continues to underperform, I wouldn't expect massive drawdowns, as management will likely take advantage of this opportunity to purchase even more shares for cheap.

Risk

Given that Innospec is a small-cap name with less sell-side coverage (there are even few SA analysts writing about it), the discounted valuation could persist, as improvement could easily go unnoticed. A trap like this one has caught many value investors, myself included, so I caution readers to size their investments accordingly. The second risk is the demand management expects from the Middle East. In the recent call, management stated it expects more volume shipments to the Middle East, as that market is more receptive to its DRA products and other chemicals. The ongoing war could disrupt the supply chain, which might lead to delays and, in the worst-case scenario, cancellations. Management also noted Q1 might witness more growth contraction due to the winter season that extended to January of this year, so I don't expect the catalyst event to have imminent results in the coming quarter; however, if the oilfield segment still doesn't show any recovery in the second half of this year, I will take that as an invalidation of my thesis.

Conclusion

Entering this war season, the market is presenting us with a likely beneficiary that is massively discounted with less further downside expected (supported by the share repurchase program). Innospec supplies chemicals that play a vital role in the production, refining, and delivery of oil. As the Strait of Hormuz remains closed, more wells will be forced to come online to meet demand needs as oil prices head for $150 per barrel. Part of the production process includes the purchase of chemical additives that help to increase efficiency and reduce drag and corrosion in the final product. Innospec being one of the leading suppliers of these additives, this will catalyze growth in the oilfield segment, making a perfect setup for price appreciation; hence, I rate this name a strong buy.

data.title: Small Cap Momentum Moderates
data.description:
Small cap performance moderates

Market leadership shifted modestly this week, with the performance gap between small cap and large cap stocks narrowing.

Earlier this year, the Russell 2000 Index outperformed the large cap S&P 500 Index by as much as 8 percentage points. As of March 12, that gap had narrowed to roughly 3%.

A likely driver has been increased investor caution amid developments in the Middle East. Periods of heightened uncertainty typically weigh more heavily on small cap stocks, which tend to be more economically sensitive and more volatile than their large cap counterparts.

That said, we remain broadly constructive on the outlook for small caps in 2026. Valuations remain compelling. Small cap stocks — particularly in the U.S. — continue to trade at a meaningful discount relative to large caps. In addition, our baseline outlook still calls for solid U.S. economic growth. Historically, firm economic growth has provided a tailwind for small cap equities.

In our view, maintaining exposure to small caps remains an important component of a diversified equity allocation.

Inflation data

This week brought inflation updates from both the U.S. and China. In the U.S., consumer inflation came in exactly in line with consensus expectations:

Headline CPI: 2.4% year-over-year

Core CPI: 2.5% year-over-year

Beneath the surface, the report showed encouraging details. A combined measure of wage-sensitive and shelter inflation moderated compared to January. Core inflation held steady at 2.5%, largely due to pressure from more volatile components, such as airfare.

Looking ahead, recent increases in energy prices tied to Middle East developments could present a near-term challenge for the Federal Reserve. While energy prices directly affect headline inflation, sustained increases can also feed indirectly into core inflation. For example, higher fuel costs can push up transportation prices, including airfares.

Even so, over the medium term, we continue to expect the Fed will make further progress in its inflation fight.

In China, inflation came in slightly above consensus expectations. However, given that investor concerns in China have centered more on deflation risks than overheating inflation, the modest upside surprise is not particularly troubling.

Recent growth data has also shown improvement, with both exports and imports rising meaningfully year-over-year. While structural headwinds remain — including property sector weakness and elevated youth unemployment — we expect policymakers to continue supporting growth in pursuit of their stated 4.5%–5% GDP target for 2026.

Central bank decisions ahead

Attention now turns to next week’s central bank meetings, with the Federal Reserve, the Bank of Canada and the Bank of Japan all set to announce policy decisions.

All three central banks are widely expected to leave policy rates unchanged. The more important focus will be on forward guidance — specifically, how policymakers assess the inflation outlook and evolving economic conditions.

Our baseline expectations:

Federal Reserve: Likely to remain gradual and data-dependent. One rate cut later this year remains possible, though continued economic strength could result in no cuts at all.

Bank of Canada: The bar for rate cuts remains high, but economic uncertainty and labor market pressures could create scope for easing later in the year.

Bank of Japan: We expect gradual rate hikes over time, but policymakers are likely to proceed cautiously to avoid tightening too quickly.

As markets digest incoming inflation data and geopolitical developments, central bank communication will play a critical role in shaping expectations for the remainder of 2026.

These views are subject to change at any time based upon market or other conditions and are current as of the date at the top of the page. The information, analysis, and opinions expressed herein are for general information only and are not intended to provide specific advice or recommendations for any individual or entity.

This material is not an offer, solicitation or recommendation to purchase any security.

Forecasting represents predictions of market prices and/or volume patterns utilizing varying analytical data. It is not representative of a projection of the stock market, or of any specific investment.

Nothing contained in this material is intended to constitute legal, tax, securities or investment advice, nor an opinion regarding the appropriateness of any investment. The general information contained in this publication should not be acted upon without obtaining specific legal, tax and investment advice from a licensed professional.

Diversification and strategic asset allocation do not assure a profit or guarantee against loss in declining markets.

Please remember that all investments carry some level of risk, including the potential loss of principal invested. They do not typically grow at an even rate of return and may experience negative growth. As with any type of portfolio structuring, attempting to reduce risk and increase return could, at certain times, unintentionally reduce returns.

The Russell Investments logo is a trademark and service mark of Russell Investments

The information, analyses and opinions set forth herein are intended to serve as general information only and should not be relied upon by any individual or entity as advice or recommendations specific to that individual entity. Anyone using this material should consult with their own attorney, accountant, financial or tax adviser or consultants on whom they rely for investment advice specific to their own circumstances.

Products and services described on this website are intended for United States residents only. Nothing contained in this material is intended to constitute legal, tax, securities, or investment advice, nor an opinion regarding the appropriateness of any investment, nor a solicitation of any type. The general information contained on this website should not be acted upon without obtaining specific legal, tax, and investment advice from a licensed professional. Persons outside the United States may find more information about products and services available within their jurisdictions by going to Russell Investments' Worldwide site.

Russell Investments is committed to ensuring digital accessibility for people with disabilities. We are continually improving the user experience for everyone, and applying the relevant accessibility standards.

Russell Investments' ownership is composed of a majority stake held by funds managed by TA Associates Management, L.P., with a significant minority stake held by funds managed by Reverence Capital Partners, L.P. Certain of Russell Investments' employees and Hamilton Lane Advisors, LLC also hold minority, non-controlling, ownership stakes.

Frank Russell Company is the owner of the Russell trademarks contained in this material and all trademark rights related to the Russell trademarks, which the members of the Russell Investments group of companies are permitted to use under license from Frank Russell Company. The members of the Russell Investments group of companies are not affiliated in any manner with Frank Russell Company or any entity operating under the "FTSE RUSSELL" brand.

data.title: 4 'Safer' Dividend Buys In Barron's 23 Better March Bets Than T-Bills
data.description:
Foreword

While most of this collection of Barron's Better Bets (BBB) is pricey or reveals disappointing dividends, four of the ten highest-yield Dogs with the "Safest" dividends of the BBB are ready to buy. March finds Verizon (VZ), Keycorp (KEY), Regions Financial (RF), and Kinder Morgan (KMI) as the buyable top four, living up to the dogcatcher ideal. That is, each showed an annual dividend from $1K invested exceeding its single share price.

The remaining six of the March "Safer" twelve have prices exceeding the dividend returned from $1k invested. Of the six, Truist Financial (TFC) is closest to the mark with a price within $0.05. Furthermore, Altria Group (MO) is within $1.23 of parity.

With renewed downside market pressure of 54.04%, it will be possible for all ten of the top BBB "Safer" dividend dogs to become elite fair-priced, with their annual yield (from $1K invested) meeting or exceeding their single share prices.

[See a summary of the top ten fair-priced March BBB Dogs (below actionable conclusion #21 mid-article) and the aforementioned ten as "safer" choices in the Afterword, at the bottom of this article.]

Actionable Conclusions (1-10): Brokers Expect 17.06% To 28.42% Net Gains From Top-Ten BBB Dogs By March 2027

Four of the ten top-yield BBB Dogs (shaded in the chart below) were among the top-ten price gainers for the coming year based on analyst 1-year target prices. So, this March, the 2026 yield-based forecast for BBB dogs, as graded by Wall St. wizard estimates, was 40% accurate.

Estimated dividend returns from $1000 invested in the ten highest-yielding stocks and their aggregate one-year analyst median target prices, as reported by YCharts, created the projections below. Ten probable profit-generating trades projected to March 2027 were:

Truist Financial was projected to net $284.19 based on the median of target price estimates from 22 analysts, plus dividends, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 12% under the market as a whole.

KeyCorp was projected to net $283.64, based on dividends, plus median target price estimates from 22 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 8% more than the market as a whole.

U.S. Bancorp (USB) was projected to net $259.00, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 24 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 5% greater than the market as a whole.

Best Buy Co. (BBY) was projected to net $236.87, based on the median of target price estimates from 26 analysts, plus the estimated annual dividend, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 46% greater than the market as a whole.

Regions Financial was projected to net $209.38, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 24 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 3% over the market as a whole.

Equity Residential (EQR) was projected to net $201.27 based on the median of target prices estimated by 25 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 25% less than the market as a whole.

Philip Morris International (PM) was projected to net $192.83, based on the median of target price estimates from 17 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 62% less than the market as a whole.

Conagra Brands (CAG) was projected to net $179.63, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 17 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 2% opposite the market as a whole.

UDR Inc. (UDR) was projected to net $174.69, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 22 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 29% under the market as a whole.

Mid-America Apartment Communities (MAA) was projected to net $170.58, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 25 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 21% under the market as a whole.

The average net gain in price was estimated at 21.92% on $10k invested as $1k in each of these top ten BBB stocks. This gain estimate was subject to average risk/volatility 19% less than the market as a whole.

The Dividend Dogs Rule

Stocks become dividend dogs by showing three key qualities: (1) paying reliable, repeating dividends, and (2) their prices fall. Thus, (3) yield (dividend/price) grows higher than their peers. Thus, the highest-yielding stocks in any collection become valued as "dividend dogs." More specifically, they may be, in fact, best named "underdogs," owing to their contrarian nature.

February BBB Total Returns

February BBB Broker Targets

The February, 2026 BBB 23 By Yield

Actionable Conclusion: March Top Ten BBB Stocks By Yield Ranged 4.64% To 8.25% Per YCharts

The top ten March BBB Dogs occupied six of eleven Morningstar sectors.

In first place, was the first of two consumer staple stalwarts, Conagra Brands [1]. The other placed second, Altria Group [2].

After those, a lone healthcare sector member took second place, Pfizer (PFE) [2]. Then, a lone consumer discretionary stock placed fourth, Best Buy Co [4].

The communication services sector member placed fifth, Verizon [5], followed by the first of four real estate sector dogs in the sixth and eighth, to tenth positions: Realty Income (O) [6], Mid-America Apartment Communities [8], UDR Inc. (UDR) [9], and Simon Property Group (SPG) [10]

Finally, a lone energy representative, in seventh place, ONEOK (OKE) [7] completed the March Dogs of the BBB by yield.

Dividend Vs. Price Results

The top graph above shows the relative strengths of the top ten March BBB dogs by dividend and price as of market close 3/11/2026.

BBB Prices per Dividend Dollar

The full chart above reveals twenty-three BBB dogs subjected to the same test: divide the price of each stock by the annual dividend. Thus is calculated the cost per dividend dollar for each stock. The least expensive dog of the BBB is Conagra Brands at $12.13 per dividend dollar, and the most expensive is Entergy (ETR) at $40.55 per dividend dollar.

Actionable Conclusion (21): Four of Twenty-Three BBB Dogs Show Negative Free Cash Flow Margins

A negative gap between dividend yield and free cash flow yield defines a cash-poor stock. That is, their dividend payout exceeds their cash on hand to pay dividends. Four BBB companies so infected are LyondellBasell, Federal Realty, Williams Companies, and Entergy. These are considered unsafe to buy. Furthermore, their infection explains why they are missing from the IDEAL and "safer" dividend dog reckonings.

Remember this dogcatcher yield-based stock-picking strategy is contrarian. That means rooting for (buying) the underdog is productive when you don't already own those stocks. If you do hold those stocks, then you must look for opportune pullbacks in price to add to your position to best improve your dividend yield. Plenty of pullback opportunities appear to be ahead. Dread the Fed, tariffs, trade-offs, war games, rants, and uncertainty.

Price Drops, Dividend Increases or Dividend Splits Could Get All Ten BBB Dogs Back to "Fair Price" Rates For Investors

The charts above retain the current dividend amount and adjust the share price to produce a yield (from $1K invested) to equal or exceed the single share price of each stock. As the top illustration shows, four of the top ten are already ideally priced. Three more are in striking distance of Ideal, while three others need double-digit help to cope.

The alternative, of course, could be that these companies raise or split dividends. That may be too much to ask in these highly disrupted, inflationary, recessionary, yet cash-rich, and volatile times. Mr. Market is far more effective at moving prices up or down to appropriate amounts; just watch and buy when your targeted stock price moves to a sweet spot.

Actionable Conclusions: (22-31) Broker 1-Yr. Targets Showed 10.72% To 25.19% Top Ten BBB Upsides To March, 2026; (32) Three -0.36% to -9.01% Downsiders Trailed

To quantify top-dog rankings, analyst median price-target estimates provided a "market-sentiment" gauge of upside potential. Added to the simple high-yield "dog" metrics, analyst median-price target-estimates provided another tool to dig out bargains.

Analysts Forecasted A 17.98% Advantage For 5 Highest-Yield, Lowest-Priced of 10 BBB Dogs As of March 11, 2027

Ten-top BBB dogs were culled by yield for this monthly-update. Yield (dividend / price) results as verified by YCharts did the ranking.

As noted-above, top-ten BBB dogs selected 3/11/26 by yield, represented six of eleven sectors. (Financial, Industrials, Materials, Technology, and Utilities, missed the top-ten cut.)

Actionable Conclusions: Analysts Expected 5 Lowest-Priced of the Ten Highest-Yield BBB Dogs (34) To Deliver 14.51% Vs. (35) 12.30% Net Gains by All Ten Come March 11, 2027

$5000 invested as $1k in each of the five lowest-priced stocks in the top ten BBB Dividend kennel by-yield were predicted by analyst 1-year targets to deliver 17.98% more gain than from $5,000 invested in all-ten. The fifth lowest-priced top ten yielding stock, Best Buy, showed top analyst-estimated gains of 23.69%.

The five lowest-priced BBB top-yield dogs for March were: Conagra; Pfizer; UDR; Verizon; Best Buy, with prices ranging from $16.98 to $63.15.

Five higher-priced Dow top-yield dogs as of March 11 were Realty Income; Altria; ONEOK; Mid-America Apartment, and Simon Property, with prices ranging from $64.71 to $189.56.

The distinction between five low-priced dividend dogs and the general field of ten reflected Michael B. O'Higgins' "basic method" for beating the Dow. The scale of projected gains based on analyst targets added the element of "market sentiment" to note upside potential. Analyst targets provide a present day equivalent of waiting a year to find out what might happen in the market.

Caution is needed, however, since analysts are historically just 15% to 85% accurate on the direction of change and only 0% to 15% accurate on the degree of change.

As winter blows out and the market warms, the feckless Fed-fueled slide and slithering presidential trade and war pronouncements add momentum into 2026 (with the exception of a few overly speculative and techno-meme stocks). Look for all of the top ten of twenty-three BBB Dogs to become fair-priced… slowly, but surely, or real quick!

Afterword

Buy 4 of 10 'Safer' Dividend March Dogs of the BBBTTB

Mid March 2026, 10 of the 23 BBB were tagged "safer" for dividends as they showed positive one-year returns with free-cash-flow-yields greater-than dividend-yields. Then four IDEAL of the ten "safer" showed dividend yields from $1000 invested exceeding the price of a single share.

How BBB 'Safer' Dividend Vs. Price Results Might Produce A "Fair Price" Result For Investors

The chart at the top above reveals that four dividends from $1K invested in top ten 'safer' dividend BBB dogs conform to the dogcatcher standard of dividends from $1,000 invested being equal to (or greater than) the price of a single share. Those four ideal dogs were: Verizon, KeyCorp, Regions Financial, and Kinder Morgan.

The middle chart shows the recent dividend amount, and the bottom chart adjusts that share price to produce a yield (from $1K invested) to equal the single share price of each stock; a so-called "fair price."

A market correction of about $90 could put all ten share prices and a $10 correction would put nine of these ten "safer" survivors above or equal to their dividend payouts from $1k invested.

There are, of course, other ways for dividends from $1K invested to exceed the price of a single share of stock. That would be for the boards of directors of these companies to raise dividends or make stock splits to the dogcatcher ideal level. That's a lot to ask with all the corporate funds doing so much "good" elsewhere.

BBB IDEAL "Safer" Dividends Revealed Positive Returns with Cash Flow Exceeding Dividend Yields

Stay tuned for future Dividend Dogcatcher BBB 23 Updates, out here, in public, away from the paywalled Seeking Alpha Investing Group site (formerly known as the Marketplace).

The net gain estimates mentioned above did not factor-in any foreign or domestic tax problems resulting from distributions. Consult your tax advisor regarding the source and consequences of "dividends" from any investment.

Stocks listed above were suggested only as possible starting points for your "Safer" BBBTTB dog dividend stock research process. These were not recommendations.

Disclaimer: This article is for informational and educational purposes only and should not be construed to constitute investment advice. Nothing contained herein shall constitute a solicitation, recommendation or endorsement to buy or sell any security. Prices and returns on equities in this article, except as noted, are listed without consideration of fees, commissions, taxes, penalties, or interest payable due to purchasing, holding, or selling same.

Graphs and charts were compiled by Rydlun & Co., LLC from data derived from Indexarb; YCharts; finance.yahoo.com; analyst mean target price by YCharts. Open source dog art from dividenddogcatcher.com.

data.title: Iran Conflict Triggers A Major Energy Shock
data.description:
By Seema Shah, Chief Global Strategist | Christian Floro, Market Strategist | Magdalena Ocampo, Market Strategist

Situation update

The coordinated U.S.-Israel strikes on Iran on February 28 and Iran’s subsequent retaliation have pushed the region into its most volatile military confrontation in decades. With fighting still underway, commercial transit through the Strait of Hormuz has effectively ground to a halt, sending oil flows through the chokepoint to near zero.

Damage to energy infrastructure and rapidly filling storage tanks have forced several Gulf producers to curtail output. Most consequentially, QatarEnergy has shut down the Ras Laffan facility, which accounts for roughly 20% of global LNG supply. This represents the single largest disruption to energy supply in modern history.

Unsurprisingly, oil and gas markets have become the focus of investors. Crude prices, already trending higher on geopolitical risk, have climbed from around $51/bbl at the start of 2026 to above $100/bbl, briefly topping $120/bbl early this week.1 Six-month Brent futures have climbed as well, now above $80/bbl, suggesting that investors are beginning to price in a longer conflict.

Although President Trump has signaled optimism that the conflict could be short-lived, the path forward remains highly uncertain. Ultimately, the economic impact will depend on how long shipping lanes through the Strait of Hormuz remain closed and how quickly oil and gas capacity can return after fighting subsides.

Crude oil prices

Brent, daily, dollars per barrel, 2007–present

Market reaction so far

Equities

U.S. equities have shown notable resilience. Nine trading days into the conflict, the S&P 500 is down around 3% and sits less than 5% below its late January peak.2 For context, the index fell 12–13% during the early phases of both the COVID shock and the Liberation Day episode in 2025.

There are two dynamics that likely explain the muted move:

  • Equity markets tend to discount geopolitical shocks given their historically short-lived impact on company earnings.
  • Investors expect that President Trump has limited tolerance for a prolonged energy crisis that could derail his affordability agenda in a midterm election year.

International equities, however, have reacted far more sharply. Asian and European indices are down almost 8% (USD terms), reflecting their far higher sensitivity to energy prices and, in Asia’s case, to Middle East-linked LNG flows.3

Rates and Central Banks

The most pronounced market reaction has been in rates. U.S. Treasury yields have risen as inflation concerns intensify and expectations for 2026 Fed rate cuts this year are pared back.

Moves in Europe have been even more pronounced. Bund yields have risen as markets price in ECB tightening this year, while UK gilt yields have surged as investors rapidly unwind expectations for 2026 rate cuts and begin pricing the risk of hikes.

Market-implied policy rates for December 2026

Movement in expectations between February 27 and March 12

U.S. Dollar

After a period of depreciation, the dollar has reasserted its safe haven role. Its recent outperformance reflects the U.S.’s relatively stronger macro position compared with major net energy importers such as Europe and Japan.

The key tail risk: Prolonged disruption to the Strait of Hormuz

A sustained disruption in the Strait would represent a severe shock to global energy markets:

  • LNG: Roughly 20–25% of global LNG exports pass through Hormuz, primarily from Qatar and the UAE, with about 80% destined for Asia. No meaningful pipeline alternatives exist.
  • Oil: Around 30% of global seaborne oil supply transits the strait, sourced mainly from Saudi Arabia, Iraq, the UAE, Kuwait, Qatar, and Iran. Limited pipeline capacity means re-routing would still leave global supply materially short, while much of the world’s spare capacity would also be inaccessible.

There are three near-term constraints that are amplifying the shock:

  1. Commercial shipping through the Strait of Hormuz remains effectively frozen.
  2. Gulf producers have cut output as storage fills; restarting production can take weeks.
  3. Damage to infrastructure (due to Iranian attacks) implies further delays even after hostilities end.

There are mitigants, but they are modest relative to the scale. The G7’s 400-million-barrel release from Strategic Petroleum Reserves provides some relief but cannot fully offset an estimated 20mb daily supply loss. (Note: This is the largest coordinated SPR release on record and more than double the size of the drawdown that followed Russia’s invasion of Ukraine.) Following the announcement, oil prices continued to rise, underscoring both the scale of the disruption and the limited nature of buffers.

U.S. shale could help if prices remain elevated, though producers are unlikely to accelerate drilling unless the conflict looks prolonged. Additionally, while OPEC+ spare capacity can offer some buffer, storage constraints in the Gulf states sharply limit its near‑term effectiveness.

Ultimately, oil prices will depend almost entirely on when transit through the Strait of Hormuz resumes and how quickly productive capacity returns. A return to pre-conflict price levels is far from assured.

A vulnerable moment for the global economy

The global economy appears capable of absorbing a modest, temporary rise in energy prices. U.S. growth remains robust, capital expenditure is strong, and household energy spending as a share of income is near historic lows. Furthermore, corporate profit margins also remain elevated, providing a buffer against higher input costs. Europe is seeing early signs of a recovery in growth, while Asia remains a key engine of global expansion, driven by AI‑related investment, resilient tech exports, and healthy domestic demand. Additionally, energy intensity has declined, reducing the likely economic impact of energy shocks compared with the 1970s.

But vulnerabilities persist. Inflation has eased but remains above central bank targets, and a sustained rise in oil prices would pressure consumers and likely delay rate cuts that had been anticipated at the beginning of the year.

Energy intensity by region

Primary energy consumption per GDP, kilowatt-hours/$, 1975–2022

Implications for U.S. growth and inflation

Historically, oil price shocks have lowered growth and raised inflation. In the U.S., the drag on growth tends to be lagged and partially offset by higher energy investment, while inflation effects are more immediate and can propagate through broader cost channels.

Using a Federal Reserve Board model to quantify the effects of higher crude oil prices:

  • Each $10/bbl increase in oil prices reduces GDP growth by 0.1ppt and raises headline inflation by 0.3ppt after four quarters.
  • At higher price levels, oil price effects become increasingly non-linear, and risks to both growth and inflation widen.

It is also worth noting that, beyond energy, the Middle East plays a critical role in other inputs, including fertilizers (16% of global ammonia, urea, and nitrogen flows) and 25–35% of global helium, a key input in semiconductor manufacturing. As such, the growth impact of a more severe, prolonged conflict would extend beyond the oil price shock.

Given the unusually high uncertainty around both the duration of the conflict and the speed at which energy flows could normalize, it is helpful to anchor expectations around a set of plausible paths. The following scenarios map out how varying degrees of disruption to oil and LNG supply could shape U.S. growth, inflation dynamics, and policy rates, offering a sense of the thresholds at which macro conditions and market behavior could change materially.

Baseline scenario: A sharp but brief oil spike, lasting a few weeks, having negligible growth effects and lifting headline inflation by ~0.2ppt for a quarter, with little impact on core inflation and therefore Fed policy.

Medium adverse scenario: Oil stabilizing around $90/bbl for 2-3 months before only gradually declining would have a more significant impact on growth, yet would still be modest, reducing our full-year U.S. GDP growth forecast of 2.5% only slightly, given that an increase in energy capex would at least partially offset a weakening in consumer spending. On the other hand, core inflation (currently at 2.5%) would rise more significantly to around 3% by end-year, constraining the Fed’s ability to deliver the two rate cuts we currently expect.

Severe adverse scenario (tail risk): A multi‑quarter closure of the Strait of Hormuz could drive oil sustainably above $125/bbl and trigger wider supply chain risks, knocking around 0.5-1% off our 2.5% baseline forecast and pushing core inflation above 3.5% by year-end and potentially force the Fed to consider rate hikes.

Europe: More exposed, less resilient

Europe remains far more vulnerable than the U.S. to prolonged energy pressure. While direct Middle East exposure is below 2022 levels, European gas prices have nearly doubled since January. Growth risks are correspondingly higher.

Yet the regional impacts will likely be uneven. Energy-independent producers such as Norway and Sweden are better insulated, while Italy, Spain, and energy-intensive Germany are more exposed and face significantly greater downside.

Energy imports dependency rate

% of net imports in gross available energy, 2024

Investment implications

Markets are still in the early stages of digesting the conflict, and pricing remains highly dependent on assumptions about how long it will last. So far, earnings expectations remain resilient, but a prolonged energy shock would begin to erode that foundation, negatively impacting risk assets with greater severity.

With signals mixed across asset classes and risks shifting rapidly, a few themes stand out for investors as the energy shock evolves.

  1. Equities and bonds are telling different stories. Equity markets continue to price a short conflict with limited economic fallout, while bond markets are bracing for a longer lasting inflation shock. The eventual path will likely fall somewhere between the two interpretations

  2. The definition of “severe” has shifted higher. Just weeks ago, $100/bbl for oil was considered a tail-risk scenario. Markets must now contend with the possibility that $130/bbl is the new threshold, yet equities have barely reacted, suggesting growing complacency.

  3. Data noise is high. Investors are leaning on unreliable indicators, such as drone attack counts or informal shipping updates, which reinforce uncertainty about the duration of the conflict.

  4. Technology continues to outperform. Alongside energy, tech is the only S&P 500 sector in positive territory this month. Despite rising input cost risks for key Asian semiconductor and memory chip manufacturers, investors see valuation opportunities following February’s tech correction and view the sector’s strong balance sheets as a buffer against macro headwinds.

  5. Country and sector diversification remains critical. Historically, the U.S. outperformed during oil shocks due to its relative energy independence. Still, markets more levered to energy, defense, and inflation resilient sectors, such as Norway, Canada, Brazil, and Saudi Arabia, have often performed even better.

Ultimately, for investors, history argues against dramatic portfolio shifts in response to geopolitical events. In an increasingly fragmented global order, disciplined diversification—across regions, sectors, and assets such as gold, high quality bonds, selective commodities, and defense—remains one of the most effective ways to manage uncertainty while staying aligned with long term objectives.

Footnotes

  1. Crude spiked above $120/bbl on Monday, March 9, during early European trading hours.

  2. March 1, 2026 – March 12, 2026.

  3. March 1, 2026 – March 12, 2026.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Vaalco Energy: Lucky Break
data.description:
VAALCO Energy (EGY) entered fiscal year 2026 expecting to begin the fiscal year with significant negative cash flows. The last article noted that with the FPSO leaving the shipyard and on its way back to the production field, the worst of the expenditures was likely over. The drilling campaign in Gabon had costs first and then producing wells (for cash flow) next. But now production is climbing as the drilling campaign posts some success. The FPSO is expected to be back in the field with production restored by the second half of the year (and possibly as early as the end of May). All of this points to a second half of fiscal year 2026 that is likely to be much better than the start. It also points to a big year for cash flow improvement.

The lucky break comes from the Iranian crisis. This company operates in Western Africa with significant operations in Egypt. The issue with the Iranian crisis is the Strait of Hormuz, where probably close to 20% of the world's oil supply has to travel (through). This company has locations that avoid that situation completely. Therefore, should oil prices spike as expected, the company is expected to rake in the cash at a time when "raking in the cash" is sorely needed to keep borrowings down to a minimum.

Cash Flow

Currently the company has a net borrowing position of very roughly $1 million.

The reason the position is still pretty good is that the company made major progress in collecting Egyptian past due receivables. That process alone resulted in a bonanza in the rough range of $70 million in unexpected (but badly needed) cash income. Egypt always pays. It is just a matter of when.

Until that FPSO is back in place and production is completely restored, there is still likely to be more negative cash flow for the first and second quarters.

This is true even though the Gabon drilling campaign is successfully adding production to improve the cash flow situation.

Earnings

The fourth quarter earnings were anything but normal. The key here is the cash flow shown above. Whenever a company like this has a major project that causes negative cash flow, the major objective is to make sure that the borrowing "does not get out of hand".

That goal far exceeds any earnings report. Clearly, with a net debt position of about $1 million, this company is in an excellent financial position to get the FPSO hooked up and producing. This whole financial situation could have been much worse without the Egyptian past due collections.

There is an impairment charge that is accompanying the sale of the Canadian business. That sale was yet another step by management to keep the debt under control. The unconventional business in Canada was different from really any of the African businesses. Now the Egyptian business may use some of the techniques from that Canadian business (like horizontal wells and "modern completion techniques"). But overall, that sale probably made sense in terms of focusing the company on its profitable core business in Africa.

2026 Capital Budget

In fiscal year 2026, the paramount consideration is the continuation of two large projects begun in fiscal year 2025.

The first major expenditure is the FPSO reconnection to begin production again. The major consideration of possible cost overruns at the shipyard is now a past issue. The market knows the results of repairs and upgrades. The key here is that the company has emerged from this with a strong financial position so far.

The next big step of getting the FPSO back to the field and reconnecting the wells for production startup is considered relatively routine. But it is still a lot of money for a company of this size. Hence there will be some market fears until production restarts. When it comes to a major cash drain (like this project), nothing succeeds with Mr. Market like the end of the project and the start of cash flow.

The next big project underway is the Gabon drilling campaign. But this campaign already has some success. It is therefore already contributing to cash flow even though drilling will continue for several more months. This drilling campaign is likely to be the single most significant contributor to cash flow increases until FPSO production is restored.

Once the FPSO is back online for production, the cash flow of the company is likely to turn positive. Then debt repayment will become a priority. This company has a long history of no debt on the balance sheet. Even though the net debt position is conservative, the borrowing so far represents a significant break with past history. Investors should therefore expect a very high priority of debt repayment and a return to a large cash balance before there is any increase in shareholder returns.

Summary

The untold story here is the success in bringing Egyptian payments current at a time when that cash was sorely needed. To say that it prevented a lot of unnecessary borrowing (and hence interest expense) is an understatement.

Even if Egypt backslides, as it is prone to do from time to time, the company is at the point where positive cash flow is just on the horizon. Positive cash flow could allow one of those periodic backslides without a material change in financial position.

This is a strong buy idea because management has a fairly long-stated goal of tripling production by 2030. Production increases from the lows of this strategy actually began in fiscal year 2025. This fiscal year, production should increase throughout the fiscal year.

The restart of production in Côte d'Ivoire should mark the start of positive cash flow not long afterwards. That will mark the end of large cash expenditures to repair and upgrade the FPSO (and, of course, get it back to the field and hookups for regular production). The swing between cash outflows for the expense of that project and cash inflows from regular production should be substantial.

But that makes VAALCO Energy a strong buy idea. In this frothy market, the company is about to post some strong production comparisons, aided by an Iranian crisis that may well push commodity prices skywards for a bit. For at least the next four years, this is a growth story that is fairly rare in this industry.

I would also consider holding this idea until management sells the company or the current story changes materially.

Risks

There could be a quick end to the Iranian situation. That may well dampen the expected benefits of higher oil prices. Right now, that seems unlikely. But investors may want to use the current situation to raise cash for however long it lasts and then worry about things like strong buys later.

Even if the current situation lasts a year, that is still not all that much time to get ready for what likely follows. Right now, there is a lot of talk about repeats of scary scenarios like the 1970s and even 2008. Whether it happens is another matter entirely.

Along with that scary talk is the other side, which claims both situations could have been avoided. We will soon see if that is the case here.

What really needs to happen is the political considerations need to fade from dominance. Until that happens, fundamentals may not matter as much as politics for this industry. That could make future analysis a real challenge.
